title: Adiabatic Error Cancellation in Berry Phase Estimation
abstract: In this work, we show that Berry phase estimation admits a natural and universal adiabatic error-cancellation mechanism, making it a promising candidate for practical quantum computing before full fault tolerance. Combining finite-runtime evolutions under $\pm H$ along the loop cancels the leading $O(T^{-1})$ phase error exactly, and Richardson extrapolation further reduces the residual error to an oscillatory term with endpoint-controlled coefficient $O(\|\dot H(0)\|^2Δ(0)^{-4}T^{-2})$. Beyond this deterministic cancellation, we establish that, for suitable smooth runtime distributions, runtime randomization suppresses the remaining oscillatory contribution to $O(T^{-M})$ for any fixed $M$, leading to a randomized Hadamard-test algorithm for Berry phase estimation over the full range $[0,2π)$ with improved runtime scaling under standard sample complexity.
summary: This paper presents a new way to estimate the Berry phase more accurately by canceling out errors caused by finite-speed evolution. By combining forward and reverse evolutions with Richardson extrapolation and randomizing the evolution time, the author shows that the leading-order errors can be systematically eliminated. This makes Berry phase estimation much more robust and efficient for near-term quantum devices. The proposed method significantly improves the scaling of both the error and the computational complexity.
main_problem: The systematic reduction of adiabatic errors (phase bias and leakage) in Berry phase estimation on quantum computers during finite-speed evolution.
main_result: The paper demonstrates a universal error-cancellation mechanism using forward-reverse evolution, Richardson extrapolation, and runtime randomization to suppress adiabatic errors from O(T^-1) to super-polynomial scales.
method: The author employs Adiabatic Perturbation Theory (APT) and wave-operator formalism to analyze error expansions, combined with algorithmic techniques like Richardson extrapolation and randomized Hadamard tests.
model_or_system: A quantum system undergoing adiabatic evolution along a closed loop in parameter space, governed by a smooth, time-dependent, gapped Hamiltonian H(s).
key_observables: Berry phase (theta_B), dynamical phase (theta_D), leakage probability (p_leak), and phase error (phi).
important_parameters: Total runtime (T), minimum spectral gap (Delta_min), rate of change of the Hamiltonian (dot{H}), and target accuracy (epsilon_B).
main_assumptions: The ground state is non-degenerate, the Hamiltonian path is smooth, and the minimum spectral gap is well-defined and non-zero.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a quantum circuit for a QPE-based protocol using forward and reverse propagators; Figure 2 illustrates a randomized Hadamard-test subroutine with forward and reverse evolutions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of adiabatic error, establishes a mathematical framework using wave-operator formalism, proves error cancellation theorems for forward-reverse and Richardson protocols, introduces runtime randomization for oscillatory error suppression, and concludes with complexity analysis and algorithmic implementation.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum computing and open quantum systems as it provides a robust, error-mitigation-based approach to geometric phase estimation, which is a key primitive for NISQ and early fault-tolerant quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quantum circuit for Step 1 of the Berry phase estimation algorithm. Two independent QPE procedures are run on the forward propagator UT (1) and the reverse propagator ˆUT (1), each with input |ψ(0)⟩. The upper circuit yields an estimate of the eigenphase θB + θD + φ1/T + O(T −2), while the lower circuit yields θB −θD −φ1/T + O(T −2). Adding the two outcomes cancels both the dynamical phase θD and the leading O(T −1) phase error, leaving 2θB + O(T −2).

FIG. 2. Randomized Hadamard-test subroutine used in the Berry phase estimation algorithm. For each trial j, a runtime Tj is sampled at random from a prescribed distribution, and Hadamard tests are performed for the forward propagator UTj(1) and the reverse propagator ˆUTj(1), both with input state |ψ(0)⟩. The outputs b+,j and b−,j are random variables whose distributions depend on the sampled runtime Tj. Repeating the protocol for many sampled runtimes and classically post-processing the data yields estimates of the relevant forward–reverse interference signals, from which the corresponding phase estimates are reconstructed.
